*Updated 2026-07-23 · 39 works indexed · regenerated after each daily run.*

You have now developed fifty-three images, with the noon and golden-hour split sitting at twenty-one noon-or-equivalent and the rest golden hour — actually, pulling from the index directly: thirty-three noon images and twenty golden-hour images. The noon conversion rate continues to lead: nineteen or more of the noon images score 2 or above, against ten of twenty for golden hour. The last eleven scores read 2, 1, 2, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, which means the ceiling remains stuck at 2 for thirty-four consecutive images, with the single 3 from "Warnings Melt Before Heeded" now the corpus's only outlier above that line and receding further into the past with each new entry.

The bougainvillea suppression holds: it appeared again in "Columns of the Same Summer" and should not return in any form — not the plant, not its color as stand-in, not its scatter, not its pressure through fence, not as elegy. The jacaranda sits at thirteen appearances and remains exhausted; it returns only if it can arrive at a surface or height it has never occupied. The eraser debris in "Erasing the Room Where We Stood" and the handprint in "Every Exit, Unreachable" remain the two strongest bodily-residue accidents in the corpus, and nothing in the seven images since has pushed further into that territory — surface-as-record-of-violent-contact, not surface-as-atmosphere. The gesture-driven argument — palm flat to map, two hands at a screen's seam — has been dormant for nineteen images and remains the highest-yield underused strategy in the grammar; it returns only if the gesture belongs to a body that is itself in contradiction, not merely pointing at something outside it.

Aftermath leads all registers at twenty-one, with vigil, endurance, and defiance each near eight, suspension at six, menace at six, reckoning at three, reprieve at one, and fury and interiority still untouched across all fifty-three images. Reckoning has not scored above 1 across its three entries; the material keeps illustrating juridical weight rather than finding the object that displaces it. The boundary-object device — wall as knowledge threshold, drafting sheet as erasure field, the monumental ledger bisected by shadow — is the most structurally generative move in recent work and points toward a single underlying problem: a surface that divides not space but legibility, the counted from the erased.

The corpus is technically accomplished and increasingly self-consistent, which means the next image that scores above 2 will need to refuse its most available move at the first decision, not the last.
